artifacts.content:
Hi Danielle,

You’re probably still wrangling paint cups, but I wanted to get this down while it’s fresh in my head.

Walking past the Long Street Apartments this morning, I couldn’t stop thinking about those March–June 2020 evictions and that 6th‑grader whose family we walked through emergency shelter intake at the YMCA on Long Street. That whole stretch of months is basically why I keep pushing trauma‑informed language in our program design. I was telling Tasha that our current family‑stability and crisis‑response framework is basically built out of what we learned during those YMCA shelter stays.

It also hit me how much of this is wrapped up with our own block. When we first started seeing the opioid‑related overdoses on East Long between 2020 and 2022, from our window at 2149 East Long Street, Apartment 3B, it felt like the whole corridor was on edge. Remember that night we called 911 twice in an hour? That’s when I started talking about “stable, trauma‑informed after‑school and family learning support” for kids whose parents were literally watching overdoses outside their front doors.

Sometimes I feel like everything in my work traces back to our little triangle: East Long Street, Franklin Park, and your old church. I was thinking about June 2019 again, when you were outed as queer and basically pushed out of that conservative church community in Columbus. That’s the reason I’m so stubborn about us naming ourselves as LGBTQ+‑affirming in staff trainings. I don’t want any kid on this side of town to have to choose between a faith space and a safe learning space.

Anyway, Maya’s snack is packed for tomorrow; she insisted on the star‑shaped cucumbers. I’ll grab her from Eastgate if your staff meeting runs long.

Love,
J

metadata.subject: East Long is in everything I’m doing lately

artifacts.content:
Hey Chris,

I’ve been meaning to write a real email instead of just reacting to your memes.

I was talking with Mom last night about how much of what I care about now started back when Dad got laid off at Rickenbacker in April 2009. Remember how fast everything slid? One month he was at the warehouse in the Rickenbacker industrial park, the next we were living entirely on free school lunches from Champion Middle School and those two months of emergency utility assistance so we could keep the lights on and still do homework at the kitchen table. That’s the picture in my head whenever I say “intergenerational educational inequity” at work.

Same with the way Columbus City Schools basically wrote off Mom and Dad in the late 1970s and early 1980s. Dad keeps telling that story about the counselor who said, “Kids from your street don’t go to college.” That’s why I talk about “breaking the intergenerational cycle of educational neglect” and keeping our kids off those Franklin County Juvenile Court truancy dockets.

And then there’s you. I still think about June 2019 when you went up to Noble Correctional Institution for that 18‑month sentence. Every time we have a student whose parent is locked up, I see your face in their file. I keep wanting to say that out loud at staff meetings, but I also want to respect your privacy.

On a lighter note: Maya asked if “Uncle Chris’s Sullivant Avenue is on the moon or on Earth,” so you have clearly become a mythical figure. She wants you to take her to COSI next time you’re in Columbus.

Text me your schedule for March?

Love,
Jordan

metadata.subject: Why all this work stuff feels so personal

artifacts.content:
Title: Mission + personal boundaries – brain dump

Date: 2/6/25

I keep circling around how much of my work at Bright Futures is tangled up with my own stuff.

1. Me: non-binary, multiracial (Black and White), first serious panic attacks at OSU in October 2010 after that week of racist flyers in the Union. Started sliding‑scale counseling at Riverside Mental Health Clinic because it was the only place I could afford. That’s why I care so much about “relationship‑centered spaces” and kids having somewhere to process identity and racism.

2. Maya: fall 2023 anxiety episodes, the evaluation at Nationwide Children’s Hospital, and now all those IEP meetings at Eastgate Elementary (esp. the October 2023 one with Ms. Lang). Sitting in a 45‑minute IEP meeting twice a month makes it impossible to pretend this is abstract. I want every family to have advocacy support, not just whoever has a grant writer for a parent.

3. Work missteps: August 2023 informal HR warning at the Bright Futures East Long Street site after that parent said I “overshared” my coming‑out story with their 10‑year‑old during mentoring. That stung, but it also forced me to think about how to train staff to be affirming without centering themselves.

Need to remember: these experiences can guide the work without becoming the whole story. Also: bring this up with Monica in supervision, not in front of the whole staff.

To‑do: schedule a walk after dinner (Long → Taylor loop) at least 4x/week so this doesn’t just live in my head.

Mom,

Just got home from Reynoldsburg and wanted to write while everything’s still clear in my mind.

Talking with you about Dad today reminded me again of how much has piled on him since the February 2022 layoff from the Honda plant in Marysville. Unemployment paperwork, the SNAP recertifications, those behind‑on‑rent notices you showed me last year – it’s a lot. No wonder he’s so tired on top of recovering from the stroke.

I also keep going back to your stories about Columbus City Schools in the late 1970s and early 1980s – teachers telling you and Dad you were “not college material,” the way no one intervened when you were skipping. That’s the backdrop in my head when I’m trying to keep our kids off Franklin County Juvenile Court truancy dockets. It all feels like one long timeline.

And now we’re on the other side of the table with Maya. Since fall 2023, with her anxiety evaluation at Nationwide Children’s Hospital and the IEP process at Eastgate Elementary, I finally understand what you meant about feeling talked over in meetings. That October 2023 IEP meeting with Ms. Lang was the first time I’ve ever had to say, “Can we slow down so I can actually process this?”

On a totally different note, Maya drew a picture of you at the YWCA Family Center on East Broad Street, even though she’s never been there. She says “Grandma helps kids get houses.” I think your part‑time intake work there since January 2021 has imprinted on all of us.

We’ll be back out next Sunday. I’ll text when we leave Columbus.

Love,
Jordan

Event: Truancy + attendance reflection coffee

Date: 2025-02-14
Time: 8:00–9:15 a.m.
Location: Corner Table Coffee, East Long Street
Attendees: Jordan, Danielle

Notes:

Use this time with Danielle to debrief everything around school attendance before spring conferences start.

– Revisit August 2022, when Danielle had to appear at Franklin County Juvenile Court downtown for that truancy hearing about Maya’s inconsistent kindergarten attendance at Eastgate Elementary. What did we learn from that? How did it feel walking into court over a 5‑year‑old’s absences in the 43203/43205 corridor?

– Talk about how that hearing pushed me to start mapping our service area around the Near East Side, South Side, and Hilltop neighborhoods of Columbus in Franklin County. Those Columbus City Schools stats on youth suspensions and juvenile court referrals in 43203 and 43205 were brutal.

– Connect that to the Champion Middle School parent we supported in March 2022 who had to pay a $300 juvenile court truancy fine instead of enrolling their kid in extended‑day supports. That story still bothers me.

– Also want to check in with Danielle about how she’s feeling now that I’m more openly non‑binary at Maya’s school (thinking back to the September 2022 conversation with Ms. Harris at Eastgate about my they/them pronouns) and whether she’s noticed any shift in how staff talk to us.

Remember to leave by 9:15 so I can be at the Bright Futures office on Maplewood by 9:30 for the donor call.

metadata.title: Truancy + attendance reflection coffee
metadata.location: Corner Table Coffee, East Long Street

artifacts.content:
Thread: Jordan & Tasha (BF family liaison)

2/4/25, 9:12 p.m.

Jordan: You still awake? My brain is doing that thing again.

Tasha: Unfortunately yes lol. What’s up?

Jordan: Just replaying March 2024 in my head – those three program families who got eviction notices from Franklin County Municipal Court and ended up storing their stuff in our multipurpose room. I keep seeing the pile of plastic totes next to the art supplies.

Tasha: Same. That was rough. But we kept the kids coming to program.

Jordan: Yeah. It’s all tangled up with my own memories – Dad’s October 2008 eviction on East Long when we were three months behind on the $875 rent. Near East Side trauma bingo.

Tasha: That’s literally why you fight so hard for housing‑stability language in our work.

Jordan: True. And then this past year with Maya’s IEP at Eastgate Elementary – those 45‑minute IEP meetings twice a month since Fall 2023 with Ms. Lang – I’m realizing how much families are juggling at once: housing, court, school.

Tasha: You’re allowed to feel that. Also, you need sleep.

Jordan: Lol. Before I crash: remember the March–June 2020 Long Street Apartments evictions and that 6th‑grader whose family we walked through YMCA shelter intake on Long? That’s when we should’ve started writing a real crisis‑response framework.

Tasha: We kind of did, just in our heads.

Jordan: Fair. Okay, I’m putting my phone down. See you at Maplewood tomorrow.

Tasha: Night. Breathe.

Hi Monica,

Quick debrief before tomorrow’s check‑in.

I’ve been thinking a lot about how our geographic focus and mental‑health partnerships have evolved. Growing up on the Near East Side and watching Dad get evicted from our East Long Street apartment in October 2008, when we were three months behind on the $875 rent, is a big part of why I keep pushing us to prioritize the 43203 and 43206 census tracts with the highest eviction filing rates. That’s also why the March–June 2020 Long Street Apartments evictions and the YMCA emergency shelter intakes with that 6th‑grader hit me so hard – it felt like history repeating itself.

Layered on top of that is what we’re living with Maya now. Since her anxiety evaluation at Nationwide Children’s Hospital in fall 2023 and the ongoing IEP process at Eastgate Elementary (especially the October 2023 meeting with Ms. Lang), I’ve been more aware of how many of our kids are navigating behavioral health crises with very little support. It makes me grateful we’re getting more referrals from Nationwide and OSU Wexner Medical Center for our Near East Side, South Side, and Hilltop sites.

I also want to revisit how we talk internally about inclusive, gender‑affirming spaces. Coming out as non‑binary to Ms. Harris at Eastgate in September 2022 and having to advocate for my pronouns in school emails about Maya made me realize how much our caregivers carry into the building.

None of this needs to be front‑and‑center in donor conversations, but it’s the context I’m holding when I’m drafting language or pushing for certain neighborhoods.

See you at 10 a.m. on Maplewood.

Best,
Jordan
